questions.passage:
I spent three years training my nose before I could reliably tell a rose absolute from a rose otto, and in that time I learned less about flowers than about the poverty of the words we have for what they do to us. Scent is the sense we have never learned to speak. We can name a thousand shades between scarlet and crimson, distinguish a fifth from a fourth by ear, describe a texture down to its grain, but set a smell before most people and they fall back on the crudest gestures, pleasant or foul, or worse, name the thing that made it rather than the sensation itself. It smells like roses, we say, which tells you nothing you did not already know from the vase.

The perfumer's trade begins by refusing that shortcut. In the workshop where I trained we spoke of a fragrance in three unfolding acts. The top notes are what greets you first and dies within minutes, the bright citrus or sharp green that announces a scent and then withdraws. Beneath them the heart opens over the following hour, the floral or spiced body that most people think of as the perfume itself. And under everything lie the base notes, the resins and woods and animal warmths that surface last and linger longest, the part that clings to a scarf a day later. A perfume is not a smell but a sequence, a thing that happens over time on the warmth of a particular skin, and no two skins tell the story quite the same way.

This structure gave me a vocabulary, and the vocabulary changed what I could perceive. Before I had the words top, heart, and base, a fragrance arrived as a single undifferentiated impression, agreeable or not. After, I could follow it as it moved, catch the moment the citrus surrendered to the rose, notice the cedar waiting underneath from the first. I do not think I imagined the layers into being; they were always there, doing their work below the threshold of a language that had no hooks to hang them on. What the training gave me was not a sharper nose so much as a finer net.

And yet I have come to distrust my own fluency. The categories that let me perceive so much also began, after a while, to perceive for me. I would lift a strip and my mind would race ahead to file it, bergamot top, jasmine heart, oakmoss base, before I had finished actually smelling the thing, as though the naming had become a substitute for the attention it was meant to serve. The apprentice smells a perfume; the expert smells a classification. I had gained a grammar and, without noticing, had begun to read only the grammar and not the sentence.

So I try now to hold the two in balance, to keep the vocabulary that opened the sense while resisting the reflex that closes it. When a scent is truly new I make myself wait before I reach for a category, to let the thing arrive on its own terms first and be named only afterward. It is harder than it sounds. The words we learn in order to perceive have a way of hardening into the only things we can perceive, and the ones for smell, so hard to come by, are the most precious and the most treacherous of all. I trained my nose to speak, and I am still learning when to let it fall silent.
questions.stem: The central reflection of the passage is that

choices.A: the author regrets ever having trained as a perfumer
choices.B: the human sense of smell is too primitive to reward any serious training
choices.C: naming a perfume by its top, heart, and base notes is the only correct way to perceive it
choices.D: learning a vocabulary for scent both opened the author's perception and threatened to replace it

explanations.A: The author values the training for opening the sense and seeks only to balance its dangers, expressing no regret at having trained.
explanations.B: The author treats smell as richly perceptible once trained, gaining a finer net rather than dismissing the sense as primitive.
explanations.C: The three-note structure is praised as a useful vocabulary, but the author explicitly comes to distrust reaching for it too quickly, so it is not endorsed as the only correct way to perceive.
explanations.D: Correct. The author recounts how the vocabulary of top, heart, and base gave a finer net for perceiving scent, then warns that the same categories began to perceive for me, replacing attention with classification. Holding those two effects in balance is the passage's core reflection.

questions.stem: When the author says most people 'name the thing that made it rather than the sensation itself,' the point is that

choices.A: people are usually simply mistaken about which particular object actually produced a given smell in the first place
choices.B: ordinary scent talk substitutes the source of a smell for the experience of smelling it
choices.C: the true names of smells are secrets known only to trained perfumers
choices.D: smells cannot be described in words under any circumstances

explanations.A: The complaint is not that people misidentify the source but that naming the source stands in for describing the experience; accuracy about the object is not the issue.
explanations.B: Correct. The author's example, saying a scent smells like roses, is faulted for telling you nothing you did not already know from the vase; it points to the source rather than describing the sensation. That substitution is exactly the point.
explanations.C: The passage says trained perfumers have a fuller vocabulary, not that the real names are secret; the author freely shares the top-heart-base scheme.
explanations.D: The author demonstrates that smells can be described, having learned to follow a fragrance as it unfolds; the claim is about a common shortcut, not impossibility.

questions.stem: The extended account of top, heart, and base notes functions primarily to

choices.A: show how a structured vocabulary let the author perceive a fragrance as an unfolding sequence
choices.B: prove that all perfumes contain exactly the same three ingredients
choices.C: argue that base notes matter more than the top or the heart
choices.D: explain in practical detail how commercial perfumes are manufactured, bottled, and then marketed to the public

explanations.A: Correct. The three-note description sets up the author's claim that having the words top, heart, and base changed what could be perceived, allowing a fragrance to be followed as it moved rather than met as a single impression. It grounds the passage's argument about vocabulary and perception.
explanations.B: The passage describes a structure of unfolding acts, not a fixed set of identical ingredients; it says no two skins tell the story the same way.
explanations.C: The author gives each layer its role and never ranks base notes as more important; base notes simply linger longest.
explanations.D: The account concerns how a scent is perceived over time, not the commerce of manufacture and sale, which the passage does not discuss.

questions.stem: The remark that 'the apprentice smells a perfume; the expert smells a classification' conveys the author's worry that

choices.A: classification always yields a more accurate account of a scent than raw smelling
choices.B: apprentices are incapable of perceiving anything beyond a single impression
choices.C: expertise can replace direct perception with the reflex of sorting into categories
choices.D: only experts are entitled to describe fragrances at all

explanations.A: The author distrusts the rush to classify precisely because it can crowd out perception, so classification is not being praised as more accurate.
explanations.B: The contrast credits the apprentice with smelling the perfume directly; it does not portray apprentices as unable to perceive.
explanations.C: Correct. The line dramatizes the author's fear that the naming had become a substitute for the attention it was meant to serve, with the mind racing to file a scent before finishing smelling it. Expertise threatens to swap perception for classification.
explanations.D: The passage laments how few words exist for smell and wishes perception open to all; it does not restrict description to experts.

questions.stem: The author concludes by trying to 'hold the two in balance.' The two things to be balanced are

choices.A: the rose absolute and the rose otto that the author spent three long years first learning to tell apart
choices.B: the vocabulary that opens perception and the reflex that lets naming close it off
choices.C: the commercial and the artistic demands of the perfumer's trade
choices.D: the top notes and the base notes of a single fragrance

explanations.A: The rose absolute and rose otto appear only as the early training exercise; they are not the two forces the conclusion sets in balance.
explanations.B: Correct. The closing paragraph names the balance directly: to keep the vocabulary that opened the sense while resisting the reflex that closes it, waiting to name a scent so the thing can arrive on its own terms first.
explanations.C: Commerce versus art is never the author's theme; the tension is between perceiving and naming, not between money and craft.
explanations.D: Top and base notes are parts of the fragrance's structure, not the opposed pull between open perception and premature classification the author must balance.

questions.stem: The passage most strongly supports which general claim about language and perception?

choices.A: Sensations become real only once a culture supplies names for them
choices.B: A richer descriptive vocabulary can only ever improve one's perception of a thing and never once constrain it
choices.C: Perception is entirely independent of the words available to describe it
choices.D: Words learned in order to perceive can harden into the limits of what one perceives

explanations.A: The author says the layers were always there below a language that lacked hooks for them, so sensations do not become real only when named.
explanations.B: The passage insists vocabulary can also constrain, as when naming becomes a substitute for attention, so an unqualified benefit misses the author's warning.
explanations.C: The author reports that acquiring words genuinely changed what could be perceived, contradicting the idea that perception is independent of language.
explanations.D: Correct. The author states the moral outright: the words we learn in order to perceive have a way of hardening into the only things we can perceive. The passage's whole arc, from finer net to reflexive filing, supports this double edge.
